meta.title: Solar System
meta.subject: general
meta.description: Explore the planets, moons, stars, and other wonders of our solar system.

items.back: The Sun is a star — a giant ball of hot gas at the centre of our solar system. It gives us light and heat. Without the Sun, there would be no life on Earth.

items.front: How many planets are in our solar system?
items.back: There are 8 planets: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune. Pluto used to be counted but is now called a dwarf planet.
items.hint: Count on two hands — minus two

items.front: 🌍 Which planet do we live on?
items.back: We live on Earth — the third planet from the Sun. Earth is the only known planet with liquid water on its surface and life.
Jupiter is the biggest planet. It is so large that all the other planets could fit inside it! It has a huge storm called the Great Red Spot.
items.hint: It's named after the king of Roman gods

items.back: Saturn is famous for its beautiful rings made of ice and rock. It also has 146 known moons — the most of any planet!
items.hint: It looks like it's wearing a hat

items.back: The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite. It orbits the Earth and takes about 27 days to complete one orbit. We can see it at night because it reflects sunlight.

items.front: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
items.back: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun. It is also the smallest planet. A day on Mercury is longer than its year!
Neptune is the farthest planet and the coldest, with temperatures as low as −214°C. It has the strongest winds in the solar system.
A meteor is a piece of rock or metal from space that burns up as it enters Earth's atmosphere. We sometimes call it a 'shooting star'.
items.hint: It streaks across the night sky

items.back: A galaxy is a huge collection of billions of stars, gas, and dust held together by gravity. Our solar system is inside the Milky Way galaxy.

items.question: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
items.explanation: Mercury is the first and smallest planet in our solar system, orbiting closest to the Sun.

items.question: Which is the largest planet in our solar system?
items.explanation: Jupiter is the largest planet — all other planets could fit inside it!
items.hint: Named after the king of gods

items.question: How many planets are in our solar system?
items.explanation: There are 8 planets: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune. Pluto is now classed as a dwarf planet.
items.hint: Pluto was removed from the list

items.question: Which planet is known for its beautiful rings?
items.explanation: Saturn has stunning rings made of billions of pieces of ice and rock.
items.hint: It looks like it has a hula hoop

items.question: What is the Sun made of?
items.explanation: The Sun is a giant ball of hot plasma, mainly hydrogen and helium, held together by gravity.

items.question: Which planet do humans live on?
items.explanation: Earth is the third planet from the Sun and the only known planet with life.

items.question: What do we call Earth's natural satellite?
items.explanation: The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite. It orbits Earth every 27 days.
items.hint: You can see it at night

items.question: Which planet is called the Red Planet?
items.explanation: Mars appears red because of iron oxide (rust) on its surface.

items.question: What is a shooting star really?
items.answer: A meteor burning up in the atmosphere
options: A meteor burning up in the atmosphere
items.explanation: A 'shooting star' is actually a meteor — a rock from space that burns up as it enters Earth's atmosphere.

items.question: What is our solar system's galaxy called?
items.explanation: Our solar system is part of the Milky Way galaxy, which contains hundreds of billions of stars.
items.hint: You can see it on a clear night

items.question: Which planet has the most moons?
items.explanation: Saturn has 146 known moons, the most of any planet in our solar system.

items.question: What keeps planets orbiting the Sun?
items.explanation: The Sun's gravity pulls planets toward it, while their speed keeps them moving in an orbit — a perfect balance.
items.hint: The same force that keeps you on the ground

items.question: Which planet spins on its side?
items.explanation: Uranus has an extreme axial tilt of 98° — it essentially rolls around the Sun on its side.

items.question: How long does light from the Sun take to reach Earth?
items.explanation: Light travels at 300,000 km/s. The Sun is 150 million km away, so light takes about 8 minutes to reach us.
items.hint: Faster than anything, but still takes a few minutes

items.question: Which is the hottest planet?
items.explanation: Venus is hotter than Mercury despite being farther from the Sun. Its thick atmosphere traps heat like a blanket.
items.hint: Not the closest, but the hottest

items.question: What is a comet made of?
items.explanation: Comets are 'dirty snowballs' made of ice, dust, and rock. As they approach the Sun the ice melts, creating a bright tail.
items.hint: Think of a dirty snowball in space

items.question: Which planet has the Great Red Spot?
items.explanation: The Great Red Spot on Jupiter is a storm larger than Earth that has been raging for hundreds of years.

items.question: What is the order of the first four planets from the Sun?
items.explanation: Remember: My Very Educated Mother — for Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars.

items.question: What is the asteroid belt?
items.answer: A region of rocky objects between Mars and Jupiter
options: A region of rocky objects between Mars and Jupiter
A belt of comets near Neptune
items.explanation: The asteroid belt lies between Mars and Jupiter and contains millions of rocky objects left over from the formation of the solar system.
items.hint: It sits between the inner and outer planets

items.question: How long does Earth take to orbit the Sun once?
items.explanation: Earth completes one full orbit around the Sun in 365.25 days — which is why we have a leap year every 4 years.
